title: Listening - Part 3: Improving a Town
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a change they would like to see in their town. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for suggesting the change. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a change they would like to see in their town. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for suggesting the change. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five residents talk about changes they would like in their town.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: The town council encourages us to recycle, but in my block of flats there are bins only for general rubbish. Anyone wanting to recycle glass or cardboard has to drive to a centre outside town, which many residents simply don't do. [19]I'd like clearly labelled recycling containers placed near apartment buildings, so people could sort their waste without making a special journey.[/19] I'm sure far more material would be recycled if the system were convenient for people who don't have cars or much storage space at home.||Speaker 2 Man: I cycle to college most days, but there is one main road where bikes share a narrow lane with buses and cars. Several friends have stopped cycling after frightening experiences there. [20]The town should create a separate cycle path on that route, because people need to get across town without feeling they might be knocked off their bikes by passing traffic.[/20] It might encourage more people to cycle as well, but my first concern is that those already doing so can travel safely.||Speaker 3 Woman: My younger brother is fifteen, and after school there is almost nowhere for him and his friends to go unless they play organised sport. They end up walking around shopping areas, where adults complain about them. [21]An indoor youth centre with music, games and affordable workshops would give teenagers somewhere positive to spend their free time rather than simply being told not to gather in public places.[/21] The town provides activities for young children and older adults; teenagers deserve something designed for them too.||Speaker 4 Man: My mother uses a wheelchair, and she enjoys shopping locally, but several older shops still have steps at the entrance. Even the town hall's side entrance involves a long route around the building. [22]I would like grants and stricter rules to ensure public buildings and shops have ramps or suitable entrances, so wheelchair users can enter independently like everyone else.[/22] Improving appearances is less important to me than removing barriers that prevent some residents from using ordinary local services.||Speaker 5 Woman: There's an old cinema in our town that closed years ago. The roof now leaks, and developers have suggested replacing it with flats. It may not look impressive at present, but generations of local families remember going there. [23]I think the building should be repaired and reused rather than knocked down, because once an important part of the town's history disappears, it can never be replaced.[/23] It could become a community arts space, but saving the building itself is the priority.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: The town council encourages us to recycle, but in my block of flats there are bins only for general rubbish. Anyone wanting to recycle glass or cardboard has to drive to a centre outside town, which many residents simply don't do. I'd like clearly labelled recycling containers placed near apartment buildings, so people could sort their waste without making a special journey. I'm sure far more material would be recycled if the system were convenient for people who don't have cars or much storage space at home.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I cycle to college most days, but there is one main road where bikes share a narrow lane with buses and cars. Several friends have stopped cycling after frightening experiences there. The town should create a separate cycle path on that route, because people need to get across town without feeling they might be knocked off their bikes by passing traffic. It might encourage more people to cycle as well, but my first concern is that those already doing so can travel safely.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: My younger brother is fifteen, and after school there is almost nowhere for him and his friends to go unless they play organised sport. They end up walking around shopping areas, where adults complain about them. An indoor youth centre with music, games and affordable workshops would give teenagers somewhere positive to spend their free time rather than simply being told not to gather in public places. The town provides activities for young children and older adults; teenagers deserve something designed for them too.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: My mother uses a wheelchair, and she enjoys shopping locally, but several older shops still have steps at the entrance. Even the town hall's side entrance involves a long route around the building. I would like grants and stricter rules to ensure public buildings and shops have ramps or suitable entrances, so wheelchair users can enter independently like everyone else. Improving appearances is less important to me than removing barriers that prevent some residents from using ordinary local services.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: There's an old cinema in our town that closed years ago. The roof now leaks, and developers have suggested replacing it with flats. It may not look impressive at present, but generations of local families remember going there. I think the building should be repaired and reused rather than knocked down, because once an important part of the town's history disappears, it can never be replaced. It could become a community arts space, but saving the building itself is the priority.

options.text: to protect a local historic building
to reduce noise in the town centre
to make it easier to recycle
to improve access for disabled people

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker wants nearby recycling facilities so residents can recycle more conveniently.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker proposes a separated cycle path chiefly to protect cyclists from traffic.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker wants a youth centre to provide constructive leisure opportunities for teenagers.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The speaker wants public premises to be accessible to people who use wheelchairs.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The speaker wants to prevent an old cinema with local historic importance from being demolished.
